evidence: broadcast/state.go + broadcast/loop.go build runtime callback objects from small wrappers (`getClientManagerFn`, `constructSystemInfoFn`, `preserveSystemFn`, etc.) then pass through to helpers without domain policy per layer

startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go, importer/importer.go, shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go, and docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go all define large callback bundles + merge/resolve + default/validation plumbing for one-off adapter wiring

goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go delegates almost all behavior to `wifiRuntime` callbacks and returns through runtime methods, while `wifi_runtime_methods.go` mostly defines thin adapters over external commands

config/config_update_registry.go introduces deep generic builders (`confPatchFieldSpec`, `confPatchSectionBuilder`, `merge*Ref`) for field registration, and `allConfPatchFields()` is a long, repetitive mapping table

README describes contract governance primarily as documentation and CI policy ("Runtime Contract Gate" + required test paths), while `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` and `goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go` only define contract metadata and perform no in-code policy validation.

The listed contract catalog files expose the governance surface (`ContractID`, `ActionToken`, descriptors), but there is no local, executable invariant in these files that requires lifecycle/compatibility assertions to be updated together with contract additions.

goseg/rectify/rectify_services.go: Reconcile performs service discovery, policy resolution, state mutation, persistence calls, and transition publication in one large control block (lines ~88-166), including closure-managed publish bookkeeping.

goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go, goseg/leak/handler_test.go, goseg/leak/broadcast_test.go, goseg/importer/importer_test.go are each reported with multi-cluster responsibility_cohesion in scan metadata (disconnected function clusters), indicating mixed concerns inside single test files.
goseg/importer/importer_test.go also retains high decomposition burden (large LOC/complexity) and multiple unrelated test clusters, which increases upkeep cost when test scenarios evolve.
This is a repeated pattern where a single file becomes the home for orchestration-like flow plus detailed behavior assertions, weakening local readability and test intent signaling.

findings.summary: Broadcast runtime layering is overly indirection-heavy and global-state bound, reducing reuse and creating hard-to-test coupling for loop orchestration.
evidence: `state.go` keeps a package-level singleton runtime via `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime = NewBroadcastStateRuntime()` and `resolveDefaultBroadcastStateRuntime()`
`loop.go` keeps package globals for control (`loopRunning`, `loopStopChannel`) and starts/stops a shared goroutine state machine from package-scope functions

`newBroadcastLoopRuntime` mostly stores closures that forward to functions in `state.go` (`constructSystemInfoWithRuntime`, `constructPierInfoWithRuntime`, `constructAppsInfoWithRuntime`) with no additional policy in the loop layer
findings.suggestion: Replace package-level singleton/control state with an explicit loop coordinator object returned by constructor, so each caller can own an isolated loop runtime and channel set; keep only dependency interfaces needed for actual behavior (e.g., state source and ticker policy) and collapse pure forwarding closures into direct method calls.

findings.summary: Multiple subsystems duplicate broad callback-runtime bundles that act as DI boilerplate rather than cohesive abstractions.
evidence: startuporchestrator defines nested runtime structs and per-feature resolver/validation paths (`startupInitRuntime`, `startBackgroundServicesRuntime`, `validate`, `default...`, `WithDefaults`-style wiring)
importer splits runtime into `importerStorageRuntime`, `importerUploadRuntime`, `importerProvisionRuntime` with `validateImporter*Runtime` guards and per-capability defaults
`shipworkflow` runtime embeds multiple orchestration runtime structs and resolves through `resolveWorkflowRuntime` for most operations
`docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` exports many broad runtime structs (`RuntimeContainerOps`, `RuntimeUrbitOps`, `RuntimeStartupOps`, etc.) that mostly mirror existing package functions
findings.suggestion: Define smaller, behavior-based seam interfaces at real boundaries (config/io, process lifecycle, transport) and inject concrete adapters per subsystem; eliminate repeated global validation layers by using one composition root per bounded context (startup/importer/workflow) with explicit required capabilities.

findings.summary: Config patch abstraction is over-generic and repetitive, making intent hard to read and increasing regression risk for each new field.
evidence: `config_update_registry.go` introduces several generic helper layers (`confPatchFieldSpec`, `confPatchSectionBuilder`, `confPatchFieldBinding`, `merge*Ref`) plus dozens of thin merge helpers
new field registration in `allConfPatchFields()` is highly repetitive and mixes parse, validation, merge, and assignment concerns in a single chained DSL
several merge rules differ only by selector predicates (`mergeStringSetIfNonEmptyRef`, `stringSetDirectRef`, `intSetIfNonZeroRef`, `mergeSliceWhen`) while the call sites remain verbose
high mention count in wide-params report for config files indicates frequent parameter/context bundling rather than direct struct-driven operations
findings.suggestion: Replace the generic builder-heavy DSL with a smaller, explicit registry format (or generated descriptor) where each patch key maps to one parser, one merge strategy, and one apply path; this will lower abstraction depth and centralize error semantics.

findings.summary: Contract governance relies on README/CI policy text rather than executable checks in the contract package.
evidence: README enumerates contract quality obligations in narrative form (runtime contract gate, required checks, and file list), but the contract definition files themselves do not include executable assertions that validate lifecycle-policy, compatibility, or descriptor integrity when a contract constant is changed.
This makes governance drift-prone: a change can satisfy doc updates or CI expectations in isolation, yet still miss deterministic policy checks for these declared contracts.
findings.suggestion: Add module-level executable governance for contracts (in `goseg/protocol/contracts`, e.g. via new/updated tests plus explicit validation helpers) that assert every catalog entry has matching lifecycle/compliance metadata and deterministic invariants on every build, and make CI invoke this as a direct package contract gate instead of relying on README-only expectations.

findings.summary: Startram reconcile path interleaves orchestration, policy checks, mutation, and persistence in one monolithic routine.
evidence: goseg/rectify/rectify_services.go:88-166 loops over all urbits, refreshes config, computes desired remote/service state, mutates local config and persist flags, and emits transitions from the same function.

The routine also manages local side-effect flags (`modified`, `persistNetwork`, `persistWeb`, etc.) and publishes a completion transition in the same block, making behavior ordering hard to reason about and easier to regress.
findings.suggestion: Split `Reconcile` into explicit stages: load/deduplicate targets, build reconciled desired state per urbit, apply policy updates, and then execute persistence/notification as a separate phase with small command objects; keep each function focused and unit-testable.

findings.summary: Large test files aggregate multiple unrelated behavior groups, creating mixed responsibilities and fragile maintenance paths.
evidence: Scan metadata lists `responsibility_cohesion` warnings for multiple of these files (for example, accesspoint/accesspoint_test: 12 clusters; leak/handler_test: 9 clusters; leak/broadcast_test: 8 clusters).
importer/importer_test is flagged as both large (898 LOC, complexity 59) and multi-cluster, with tests spanning diverse flows in one file.
Repeated broad-scope helpers and setup seams in these test files increase cross-test coupling and make it difficult to isolate intention of each behavioral group.
findings.suggestion: Refactor these suites into multiple focused files by domain concern (handler contracts, state reconciliation, runtime interactions, protocol parsing), extract shared builders into `*_test.go` helpers, and keep each test file near one behavioral contract.
